title: Reading the grain, paper and board direction in bookbinding
subtitle: How machine-made paper and board have a grain, and why it controls how a book opens
excerpt: Machine-made paper is not the same in both directions. The fibres align parallel to the direction the paper moved through the paper machine, and this alignment is the grain. Bookbinding depends on grain direction: get it right and the book opens flat; get it wrong and the book warps.
sourceNotes: Grain direction is documented in Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), and is a fundamental principle across all bookbinding and paper-craft literature. This reading synthesises the standard explanation from multiple public-domain sources.

glossaryTerms.definition: The direction in which cellulose fibres are predominantly aligned in a sheet of machine-made paper or board. The grain runs parallel to the machine direction in which the paper was manufactured.
Paper in which the grain runs parallel to the long side of the sheet. Also called grain-long. This is the preferred orientation for most bookbinding signatures.
Paper in which the grain runs parallel to the short side of the sheet. Using short-grain paper for book signatures causes the pages to resist lying flat when the book is opened.

content.text: Every sheet of machine-made paper has a grain. It is invisible to the naked eye, but it determines how the paper folds, how it absorbs adhesive, and how it behaves in a finished book. Handmade paper does not have a grain in the same way because the fibres are laid down randomly in the vat; machine-made paper and board are a different matter.
Where grain comes from

In a paper machine, a dilute slurry of fibres flows onto a moving mesh belt. The forward motion of the belt causes most fibres to align in the machine direction. As the water drains away, the fibres bond where they cross, but the majority are oriented along the same axis. The result is a sheet that is significantly stronger in one direction than the other, and that folds more cleanly along the grain than across it.
Testing for
grain direction
Three tests are reliable for home use.

The bend test: hold the sheet loosely over one hand and let it droop under its own weight. Note how much it droops. Turn 90 degrees and repeat. The direction in which the sheet droops less is the grain direction; the sheet is stiffer across the grain than with it.

The tear test: tear a small strip from one corner of the sheet in each direction. A tear along the grain is relatively straight and clean. A tear across the grain wanders and produces a rougher, more irregular edge.

The damp test: brush a thin line of water across one face of the sheet near an edge. The sheet will curl almost immediately, rolling along its long axis. The direction of the curl is across the grain. The fibres swell when wet and bow the sheet toward the damp face; they swell more strongly across the grain than along it.
Grain in bookbinding
In a book, the grain of all paper and board should run parallel to the spine. This means the fibres run from head to tail, parallel to the direction of opening.

When you open a page with the grain running correctly, the fibres flex easily in the direction of the opening movement. When the grain runs across the spine, the fibres resist the movement. The page stays stiff; the spine swells and weakens at the hinges. Boards made from cross-grain paper delaminate or warp as humidity changes because the paper layers in the board expand at different rates in different directions.
Checking board grain

Grey board and bookbinding board have a grain just as paper does. The bend test works: flex the board gently with both hands, first in one direction and then the other. The direction of easier bending is the grain direction. For binding boards, the grain should also run from head to tail of the finished book.
Ordering and cutting to grain
Most paper suppliers mark grain direction on the format label.
Long grain
means the grain runs parallel to the long side of the sheet.
Short grain

means the grain runs parallel to the short side. When ordering paper for a portrait-format book with a tall, narrow spine, you want long-grain sheets so the grain runs along the full height of the page.
When cutting sheets down from a larger sheet, check the grain of the parent sheet first. Orient your cuts so that every piece has its grain running in the right direction for how it will be used.
